items.title: Zmanim Documentation
items.text:
This site documents the sources and calculation methods for the zmanim included in the KosherJava library.

If the app you use is based on KosherJava, or on one of its many ports, this documentation can help explain the sources and methods used to calculate those zmanim.

This documentation was generated for KosherJava version X. The version used by your app may be different, so the information here may not match your app exactly.

items.title: Alos 120 Minutes
items.text:
Deprecated as a warning that this is a very early time. It should be used lechumra only, because using it lekula can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, or dawn, according to a very early 120-minute calculation. It should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated as 120 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting. It is based on the time to walk 5 mil at 24 minutes per mil, following the view that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 5 mil.

The calculation may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

The mil mentioned here refers to a mil.

This time depends on whether elevation is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Minutes

items.title: Alos 120 Zmanis
items.text:
Deprecated as a warning that this is a very early time. It should be used lechumra only, because using it lekula can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, or dawn, according to a very early calculation of 120 minutes zmaniyos, equal to ⅙ of the day, before sunrise. It should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated as two shaos zmaniyos before sunrise. It is based on a 24-minute mil, so 5 mil equals 120 minutes, or ⅙ of a 12-hour day. The day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset depending on the elevation setting.

The calculation may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

This time depends on whether elevation is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Zmanis

items.title: Alos 18 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, defined by the sun’s position before sunrise.

Calculated when the sun is 18° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

The calculation may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos18Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos18Degrees

items.title: Alos 19 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, defined by the sun’s position before sunrise. This is the Rambam's alos according to Rabbi Moshe Kosower's Maaglei Tzedek, page 88, Ayeles Hashachar Vol. I, page 12, Yom Valayla Shel Torah, Ch. 34, p. 222, and Rabbi Yaakov Shakow's Luach Ikvei Hayom.

Calculated when the sun is 19° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

The calculation may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Degrees

items.title: Alos 19 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, defined by the sun’s position before sunrise. It is a degree-based counterpart to the 90-minutes-before-sunrise calculation.

Calculated when the sun is 19.8° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. The 19.8° value is based on the sun’s position 90 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The calculation may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Point8Degrees

items.title: Alos 26 Degrees
items.text:
Deprecated as a warning that this is a very early time. It should be used lechumra only, because using it lekula can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, or dawn, according to a very early degree-based calculation. It should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated when the sun is 26° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This is based on the same idea as 120 minutes before sunrise, but uses the sun’s position rather than exactly 120 minutes. The 26° value is based on the sun’s position 120 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The calculation may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos26Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos26Degrees

items.title: Alos 60 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, calculated as 60 minutes before sunrise.

Calculated as 60 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting. It is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 15 minutes per mil. Time-based offset calculations are based on the view of the Rishonim that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

The calculation may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

This time depends on whether elevation is used.

The 60-minute calculation is associated with discussion of the Chavas Yair in the Mekor Chaim, Orach Chaim Ch. 90; the Chut Hashani Ch. 97; the Divrei Malkiel, Vol. 4, Ch. 20, page 34; the Maharik, Ch. 173; and the Ra'avan.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos60Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos60Minutes

items.title: Alos 72 Zmanis
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, calculated as 72 minutes zmaniyos, equal to 1/10 of the day, before sunrise. This calculation is used in calendars published by the Hisachdus Harabanim D'Artzos Habris Ve'Canada.

Calculated as 1.2 shaos zmaniyos before sunrise. It is based on an 18-minute mil, so 4 mil equals 72 minutes, or 1/10 of a 12-hour day. The day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset depending on the elevation setting.

The calculation may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

This time depends on whether elevation is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Zmanis

items.title: Alos 90 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to a fixed 90-minute interval before sunrise. It is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 22.5 minutes per mil, following the view of the Rishonim that the Neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends only on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

Calculated as 90 minutes before sunrise, using either regular sunrise or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos90Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos90Minutes

items.title: Alos 90 Zmanis
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to 90 zmaniyos minutes, equal to ⅛ of the day before sunrise. It is based on a 22.5-minute mil, so 4 mil are 90 minutes, which is ⅛ of a 12-hour day.

Calculated as sunrise, using either regular sunrise or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting, minus 1.5 GRA seasonal hours.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos90Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos90Zmanis

items.title: Alos 96 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to a fixed 96-minute interval before sunrise. It is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 24 minutes per mil, following the view of the Rishonim that the Neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends only on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

Calculated as 96 minutes before sunrise, using either regular sunrise or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos96Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos96Minutes

items.title: Alos 96 Zmanis
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to 96 zmaniyos minutes, equal to 1/7.5 of the day before sunrise. It is based on a 24-minute mil, so 4 mil are 96 minutes, which is 1/7.5 of a 12-hour day.

Calculated as sunrise, using either regular sunrise or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting, minus 1.6 GRA seasonal hours. The day is calculated from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from regular sunrise to regular sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos96Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos96Zmanis

items.title: Alos Baal Hatanya
items.text:
The Baal Hatanya's alos, dawn, defined as the time when the sun is 16.9° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is based on a 72-minute interval between dawn and netz amiti, sunrise, corresponding to walking 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil, as attributed to Rambam and others.

Calculated by the sun reaching 16.9° below the geometric zenith before sunrise. This angle is based on the sun’s position 72 minutes before netz amiti in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlosBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlosBaalHatanya

items.title: Alos 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, dawn, defined as the time when the sun is 16.1° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is based on a 72-minute interval between dawn and sunrise, and between sunset and nightfall, corresponding to walking 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil, as attributed to Rambam and others.

Calculated by the sun reaching 16.1° below the geometric horizon before sunrise. This angle is based on the sun’s position below the horizon 72 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getAlos16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getAlos16Point1Degrees

items.title: Alos 72 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to a fixed 72-minute interval before sunrise. It is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil. The 72-minute duration, but not the concept of fixed minutes, follows the view that the Neshef, the twilight between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

Calculated as 72 minutes before sunrise, using either regular sunrise or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Minutes

items.title: Bein Hashmashos
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 13 Point 24 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam, using a degree-based version of the 58.5-minutes-after-sunset calculation.

It is calculated when the sun is 13.24° below the western geometric horizon after sunset. This is based on the sun’s position 58.5 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which calculates to 13.24° below geometric zenith.

Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol. III page 1028, No. 50 says that a dip of slightly less than 13° should be used. The documentation notes 13.2456° as the proper dip, truncated to 13.24°, giving a time about 1.5 seconds earlier lechumra.

The difference between 13.24° and 13° is given as 1 minute 10 seconds in Jerusalem during the equinox, 1 minute 29 seconds in Jerusalem during the solstice, and 1 minute 56 seconds in New York during the solstice.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point24Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point24Degrees

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 13 Point 5 Minutes Before 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam in a calculation based on 13.5 minutes before shkiah calculated as 7.083°.

It is calculated as 13.5 minutes, which is ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before shkiah calculated as 7.083°.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point5MinutesBefore7Point083Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point5MinutesBefore7Point083Degrees

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 2 Stars
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam according to the opinion of the Divrei Yosef, as cited in Yisrael Vehazmanim.

It is calculated as 5/18, or 27.77%, of the time between alos, calculated as 19.8° before sunrise, and sunrise. That amount of time is added to sunset.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT2Stars

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT2Stars

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 58 Point 5 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam, calculated as 58.5 minutes after sunset.

Bain hashmashos is treated as ¾ of a mil before tzais, or 3 ¼ mil after sunset. With a mil calculated as 18 minutes, 3.25 × 18 equals 58.5 minutes after sunset.

This zman may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT58Point5Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT58Point5Minutes

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 13 Point 5 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais or nightfall starts at sunset.

It is calculated as 13.5 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

This zman may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 16 Point 875 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais or nightfall starts at sunset.

It is calculated as 16.875 minutes, or ¾ of a 22.5-minute mil, before sunset.

This zman may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

It is calculated as 18 minutes, or ¾ of a 24-minute mil, before sunset.

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 2 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais or nightfall starts at sunset.

It is calculated when the sun is 2.1° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux in Yerushalayim, corresponding to 13.5 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

This zman may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 2 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

Calculated when the sun is 2.8° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux, corresponding to 16.875 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 3 Point 05 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

Calculated when the sun is 3.05° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux, corresponding to 18 minutes, or ¾ of a 24-minute mil, before sunset.

A refraction value of 0.5166° is used lechumra, instead of the traditional 0.566°, amounting to about 14 seconds. The raw description says this is more in line with actual refraction in Eretz Yisrael and cites Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh, as well as Luach Itim Lebinah.

For more details, see The Yereim's Bain Hashmashos.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim3Point05Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim3Point05Degrees

items.title: Candle Lighting
items.text:
Candle lighting time for any day of the week, including use for Yom Tov on mid-week holidays.

Calculated as the candle lighting offset in minutes before sea level sunset.

Elevation adjustments are intentionally not included in this calculation.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getCandleLighting

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getCandleLighting

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz Baal Hatanya
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Baal Hatanya.

Calculated as 5 seasonal hours into the day, where the day is measured from netz amiti, sunrise, to sunset according to the Baal Hatanya.

This zman is only for Erev Pesach; on other days it may not be available.

It may also be unavailable if the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzBaalHatanya

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz GRA
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the GRA.

Calculated as 5 seasonal hours into the day, where the day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sunset according to the GRA.

This zman is only for Erev Pesach; on other days it may not be available.

It may also be unavailable if the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzGRA

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz MGA 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA), using dawn and nightfall defined by the sun being 16.1° below the horizon.

Calculated as 5 seasonal hours after dawn, where the day is measured from dawn to nightfall, with both defined as 16.1° below sunrise or sunset.

This zman is only for Erev Pesach; on other days it may not be available.

It may also be unavailable in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA16Point1Degrees

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz MGA 72 Minutes
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA), using dawn 72 minutes before sunrise and nightfall 72 minutes after sunset.

Calculated as 5 temporal hours after dawn, where the day is measured from dawn 72 minutes before sunrise to nightfall 72 minutes after sunset.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma 3 Hours Before Chatzos
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning, calculated as 3 regular clock hours before chatzos hayom. This is generally known as part of the “Komarno” zmanim after Rav Yitzchak Eizik of Komarno, though the description says it predates him and cites earlier and later sources.

3 regular clock hours before chatzos hayom, not shaos zmaniyos.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Alos 16 Point 1 To Sunset
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the opinion that the day starts at alos 16.1° and ends at sea level sunset. This is cited as the opinion of חידושי וכללות הרז״ה and מנורה הטהורה, as mentioned by Yisrael Vehazmanim vol. 1, sec. 7, ch. 3 no. 16.

The day is measured from alos 16.1° to sea level sunset. Three shaos zmaniyos are calculated from that day and added to alos.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

The setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is ignored for this calculation.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShmaAlos16Point1ToSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShmaAlos16Point1ToSunset

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Ateret Torah
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.

The day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset, though that sunset offset can be configured. Three shaos zmaniyos for this day are added to alos 72 zmaniyos.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to Rav Moshe Feinstein’s calculation within the GRA opinion that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset, using only the first half of the day.

The first half of the day is measured from sunrise to fixed local chatzos. Sof zman Shema is 3 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, described as half of this half-day.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Four shaos zmaniyos after dawn, where dawn is when the sun is 18° below sunrise and nightfall is when the sun is 18° below sunset.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Sof Zman Tfila MGA 72 Minutes Zmanis
items.text:
The latest time for the morning prayers according to the Magen Avraham (MGA).

It is 4 proportional solar hours after dawn. The day is measured from dawn, defined as 72 proportional minutes before sunrise, until nightfall, defined as 72 proportional minutes after sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in places like the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sof Zman Kidush Levana 15 Days
items.text:
The latest time for Kiddush Levana calculated as 15 days after the molad. This follows the opinion of the Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 426.

It is the moment 15 days after the molad.

Some opinions understand the Rema, who cites the Maharil, as using the halfway point between one molad and the next, and as saying that the Mechaber agrees. The Aruch Hashulchan is also noted. For more detail, see Rabbi Dovid Heber’s discussion in Siman Daled, chapter 4, of Shaarei Zmanim.

The time is given even if it occurs during the day. If the time falls between alos and tzais, alos is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days

items.title: Sof Zman Kidush Levana Between Moldos
items.text:
The latest time for Kiddush Levana according to the Maharil, who calculates it as halfway between one molad and the next.

It adds half of the 29 days, 12 hours, and 793 chalakim between one molad and the next to the month’s molad: 14 days, 18 hours, 22 minutes, and 666 milliseconds.

The time is given even if it occurs during the day. If the time falls between alos and tzais, alos is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

items.text:
The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to Rabbeinu Yonah, who says it can be said 3 days after the molad.

The time is given even if it occurs during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be said.

items.text:

items.title: Chatzos Hayom
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sun Transit
items.text:
Sun transit is sundial or solar noon, when the Sun is transiting the celestial meridian. See The Definition of Chatzos for details on the proper definition of solar noon or midday.

The calculation depends on the astronomical calculator being used. With the default NOAA calculation, it calculates astronomical noon. With the USNO-style SunTimes calculation, which does not calculate astronomical noon, sun transit is calculated as halfway between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset; this can be slightly off the real transit time because of changes in declination as the day lengthens or shortens.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as with the USNO-style calculation in the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunTransit

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunTransit

items.title: Fixed Local Chatzos Hayom
items.text:
Fixed local chatzos is noon adjusted from standard time to account for the local longitude. It is described as the time of chatzos according to the Aruch Hashulchan in Orach Chaim 233:14 and Rabbi Moshe Feinstein in Igros Moshe Orach Chaim 1:24 and 2:20.

The 360° of the globe divided by 24 hours equals 15° per hour, or 4 minutes per degree, so at longitudes 0°, 15°, 30°, and so on, chatzos is exactly 12:00 noon. The time is adjusted based on the local longitude relative to the nearest 15° multiple. For example, Lakewood, New Jersey, at longitude -74.222°, is 0.778° from -75°; multiplying by 4 minutes per degree gives 3 minutes and 7 seconds, for a chatzos of 11:56:53.

This calculation is not tied to theoretical 15° time zones; it adjusts to the actual time zone and Daylight saving time.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getFixedLocalChatzosHayom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getFixedLocalChatzosHayom

items.title: Chatzos Hayom
items.text:
Chatzos hayom is calculated either as astronomical chatzos or as the halfway point between sunrise and sunset, depending on the available calculation and whether astronomical chatzos is being used. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate chatzos.

When astronomical chatzos is supported and enabled, it uses astronomical chatzos. Otherwise, it uses the halfway point between sunrise and sunset. The USNO-style calculation uses halfway between sunrise and sunset, identical to six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, while the NOAA calculation uses the more accurate astronomical chatzos.

In the Arctic, halfway-between-sunrise-and-sunset chatzos may not be available on a day when either sunrise or sunset did not happen. If that happens and astronomical chatzos is supported, astronomical chatzos is used instead.

The time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, if the calculation being used does not support astronomical chatzos.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayom

items.title: Chatzos Hayom As Half Day
items.text:
This is chatzos calculated as halfway between sunrise and sunset. Many are of the opinion that chatzos is calculated as the midpoint between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, despite this not being the most accurate way to calculate it. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate chatzos.

It is calculated as halfway between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, equivalent to six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise. A day starting at alos and ending at tzais using the same time or degree offset will also produce the same time. Because the day may be lengthening or shortening, this is not necessarily the exact midpoint of the day, but it is very close.

The SunTimes calculation calculates chatzos as halfway between sunrise and sunset, while the NOAA calculation calculates astronomical chatzos, which is described as slightly more accurate.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

items.title: Midnight
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Solar Midnight
items.text:
Solar midnight is the end-of-day solar midnight for the date being calculated, which may actually be after clock midnight. For example, calculating it for February 8 refers to the midnight between February 8 and February 9. It occurs when the Sun is transiting the lower celestial meridian, or when the Sun is at its nadir.

The calculation depends on the calculator being used. With the default NOAA calculator, it calculates astronomical midnight. With the USNO calculator, midnight is calculated as 12 hours after the halfway point between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset of that day; this can be slightly off the real transit time because of changes in declination, meaning the day lengthening or shortening. See The Definition of Chatzos for details on the definition of solar noon or midday.

This time is for the Sun’s lower transit at the end of the current day.

In some situations the time may not be available, such as when using the USNO calculator in the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the Sun does not rise and one when it does not set. This is not expected with the NOAA calculator.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSolarMidnight

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSolarMidnight

items.title: Chatzos Halayla
items.text:
Chatzos halayla is the midnight point at the end of the day, and is described as the last zman of the day. It may actually be after clock midnight of the date being calculated. For example, when calculating it for Erev Pesach, it is calculated for Lail Pesach so it can be used as sof zman achilas chametz.

If astronomical chatzos halayla is supported and astronomical chatzos is enabled, astronomical chatzos halayla is used. Otherwise it is calculated as the halfway point between sunset and the following day’s sunrise. The USNO calculator calculates chatzos halayla as halfway between sunset and the following day’s sunrise, identical to six shaos zmaniyos after sunset, while the NOAA calculator calculates it more accurately as astronomical chatzos halayla. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate Chatzos.

This time is for the end of the current day.

In the Arctic, half-night chatzos may not be available on a day when either sunset or the following day’s sunrise did not happen. If that happens and astronomical chatzos is supported, astronomical chatzos is used instead.

The time may not be available in the Arctic Circle when the needed calculation cannot be computed and astronomical calculations are not supported.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHalayla

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHalayla

items.title: Mincha Gedola
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Mincha Gedola 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Magen Avraham, with the day beginning and ending when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. This is the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, using the shaah zmanis for a day based on 16.1° below the horizon. If the setting to use astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is enabled, it is calculated as half of a half-day-based shaah zmanis between chatzos hayom and alos 16.1°, added after chatzos hayom.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola16Point1Degrees

items.title: Mincha Gedola 30 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola calculated as 30 regular clock minutes after chatzos. Some use this time to delay the start of mincha in winter, when half of a shaah zmanis is less than 30 minutes. It should not be used to start mincha before the standard GRA mincha gedola. See Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim 234:1 and the Shaar Hatziyon seif katan ches.

Calculated as a fixed 30 minutes after chatzos, not as half of a shaah zmanis after chatzos.

Because this is a fixed 30 minutes after chatzos, it is affected by the setting for astronomical chatzos, and not by the separate setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one day when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola30Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola30Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola 72 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Magen Avraham, with the day beginning 72 minutes before sunrise and ending 72 minutes after sunset. This is the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, using the shaah zmanis for a day that begins 72 minutes before sunrise and ends 72 minutes after sunset. If the setting to use astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is enabled, it is calculated as half of a half-day-based shaah zmanis between chatzos hayom and tzais 72 minutes, added after chatzos hayom.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one day when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola72Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Mincha gedola based on the opinion of Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel, as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom. Mincha gedola is the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as half a shaah zmanis after chatzos, with shaos zmaniyos based on a day beginning at alos 16.1° and ending at tzais 3.7°, which is 13.5 minutes after sunset. The time used is the later of this calculation or 30 regular clock minutes after chatzos.

The setting to use astronomical chatzos for other zmanim does not apply here, because this calculation involves chatzos offset from the center of the astronomical day.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAhavatShalom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAhavatShalom

items.title: Mincha Gedola Ateret Torah
items.text:
Mincha gedola based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah. This is the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the opinion of the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos. The day begins 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset; that sunset offset can be configured to a different amount.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one day when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

items.title: Mincha Gedola Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Baal Hatanya. Mincha gedola is the earliest time one can pray mincha. The Rambam is of the opinion that it is better to delay mincha until mincha ketana, while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA, and others are of the opinion that mincha can be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola.

Calculated as 6.5 sea-level solar hours after netz amiti, or real sunrise, based on the Baal Hatanya’s view that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one day when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRAFixed Local Chatzos 30 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to Rav Moshe Feinstein, for the earliest time one can pray mincha according to the GRA.

Calculated as 30 minutes after fixed local chatzos.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one day when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRAGreater Than 30
items.text:
The later of GRA mincha gedola and the time 30 minutes after chatzos. In winter, when half of a GRA shaah zmanis is less than 30 minutes, the 30-minutes-after-chatzos time is used; otherwise, the GRA mincha gedola time is used.

Calculated by comparing GRA mincha gedola with 30 minutes after chatzos and using the later time.

Because this calculation can be based on an offset from chatzos when 30 regular clock minutes is greater than half of a shaah zmanis, it may be affected by the setting for astronomical chatzos even when the separate setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is disabled.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one day when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAGreaterThan30

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAGreaterThan30

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRA
items.text:
Mincha gedola is the earliest time one can pray mincha. This time follows the GRA. The Ramba"m says it is better to delay mincha until mincha ketana, while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA, and others say mincha may be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola.

It is calculated as 6.5 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise. The day is measured from sunrise to sunset, using either sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset or elevation-adjusted sunrise and sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRA

items.title: Mincha Ketana
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Mincha Ketana 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Magen Avraham, with the day beginning and ending when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos, using a day that starts at alos 16.1° and is divided into solar hours based on that calculation.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

items.title: Mincha Ketana 72 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Magen Avraham, with the day beginning 72 minutes before sunrise and ending 72 minutes after sunset. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos, using alos 72 minutes before sunrise and a day based on that 72-minute calculation.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana72Minutes

items.title: Mincha Ketana Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Mincha ketana based on the opinion of Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel, as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 2.5 shaos zmaniyos before tzais 3.8°, with shaos zmaniyos based on a day from alos 16.1° until tzais 3.8°.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Mincha Ketana Ateret Torah
items.text:

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos. In this calculation, the day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset.

The end of the day is usually 40 minutes after sunset, but it can be configured to a different sunset offset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text:

Calculated as half an hour before mincha ketana, or as 9 shaos zmaniyos after the start of the day. In this version, the calculation uses 9 solar hours after alos 16.1°, based on a day beginning at alos 16.1° and ending at tzais 16.1°.

items.title: Samuch Le Mincha Ketana GRA
items.text:
Samuch lemincha ketana is the time near mincha ketana, half an hour before mincha ketana. It is described as the time before which eating or other activity cannot begin prior to praying mincha.

It is calculated as 9 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after the start of the day, according to the GRA. The day starts at sunrise and ends at sunset. The sunrise used may be elevation-adjusted, depending on the elevation setting.

See the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2.

This zman may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in northern or southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.text:

items.title: Misheyakir 12 Point 85 Degrees
items.text:
This is deprecated because it is a very early misheyakir time that should only be used bish’as hadchak. Lechatchila, a later zman should be used.

A very early misheyakir time mentioned for use bish’as hadchak, based on the sun being 12.85° below geometric zenith.

Calculated for when the sun is 12.85° below geometric zenith. It is based on the sun’s position slightly later than 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This zman is mentioned for use bish’as hadchak in the Birur Halacha Tinyana and Tlisa’ah in Orach Chaim siman 18 as 12.85°.

Actual calculations show the 57-minute time to be slightly more than 12.9°, while 12.85° is described as a slight chumra compared with 12.9°, a difference of about 14 seconds at the equinox/equilux in Jerusalem.

The 57-minute based time is mentioned by the Minchas Yitzchak vol. 9, siman 9 as 15 minutes before alos hashachar, though the location is not clear and no degree-based conversion is mentioned.

The Kaf Hachaim vol. 1 siman 18, no. 18 states that in Yerushalayim 60 fixed minutes are used year round. Calculations show that 60 fixed minutes in Yerushalayim ranges from 13.5° at the spring equinox to 11.5° at the summer solstice, while 57-minute misheyakir ranges from 12.9° at the winter equinox to 11° at the summer solstice.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees

items.title: Misheyakir 7 Point 65 Degrees
items.text:
Misheyakir based on the sun being 7.65° below geometric zenith.

Calculated for when the sun is 7.65° below geometric zenith. The degree value is based on a 35/36-minute zman around the equinox / equilux, when the neshef is shortest.

This time is based on Rabbi Moshe Feinstein, who writes in Ohr Hachaim Vol. 4, Ch. 6 that misheyakir in New York is 35–40 minutes before sunrise, described as a drop less than 8°.

Rabbi Yisroel Taplin in Zmanei Yisrael notes that Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky stated that it is not less than 36 minutes before sunrise, possibly 40 minutes.

This is described as the accepted minhag in Lakewood used in the Yeshiva, following the opinion of Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who provided the time of 35/36 minutes but not a degree-based time.

Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees

items.title: Misheyakir 9 Point 5 Degrees
items.text:
Misheyakir based on the sun being 9.5° below geometric zenith.

Calculated for when the sun is 9.5° below geometric zenith. This is based on Rabbi Dovid Kronglass’s calculation of 45 minutes in Baltimore, mentioned in Divrei Chachamim No. 24 and brought in Birur Halacha, Tinyana, Ch. 18.

Rabbi Yaakov Yitzchok Neiman is cited as saying that the Vya’an Yosef did not want to rely on times earlier than 45 minutes in New York.

This zman is also used in calendars published by Rabbi Hershel Edelstein and by Edot Hamizrach communities.

This also follows the opinion of Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who provided times of 36 and 45 minutes but not degree-based times. Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees

items.text:
The time of plag hamincha according to Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.

items.text:
The time of plag hamincha according to Rav Moshe Feinstein's opinion, calculated according to the GRA.

The day ends at sunset, and plag hamincha is 4.75 shaos zmaniyos after fixed local chatzos.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaGRAFixedLocalChatzosToSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaGRAFixedLocalChatzosToSunset

items.title: Plag Hamincha GRA
items.text:
Plag hamincha according to the GRA. Plag hamincha is the earliest time that Shabbos can be started.

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise. The day is calculated from sunrise to sunset, or from sea level sunrise to sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The calculation depends on whether elevation is used: it uses sunrise and sunset when elevation is used, or sea level sunrise and sea level sunset when elevation is not used.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaGRA

items.title: Netz HaChamah
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sea Level Sunrise
items.text:
Sunrise calculated as if the location were at sea level, without any elevation adjustment.

It is the sea-level sunrise time and is used as the base for dawn calculations that are based on the sun being a certain amount below the horizon before sunrise.

Non-sunrise and non-sunset calculations such as dawn and dusk depend on visible light, which is not affected by elevation.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise

items.title: Sunrise
items.text:
Sunrise adjusted for elevation.

The calculation uses a geometric zenith of 90° plus an elevation adjustment. It is also adjusted by approximately 50/60 of a degree to account for 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total of 90.83333°.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise

items.title: Sunrise Or Easternmost Solar Azimuth
items.text:
Sunrise, if it occurs that day; otherwise, the time when the sun is at its easternmost position, an azimuth of 90°.

In polar regions, where there can be days with no sunrise or sunset, this follows an opinion that the day-night boundary during these periods is based on the sun’s easternmost or westernmost position. The corresponding sunset in this opinion is when the sun is at azimuth 270°, its westernmost position. This time is close to six hours before astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

This opinion is attributed to Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳, and is brought down lehalacha by the Ben Ish Chai in רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳.

If there is no sunrise that day and the sun does not reach azimuth 90°, this time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth

items.title: Sunset
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sea Level Sunset
items.text:
Sunset calculated as if the location were at sea level, without any elevation adjustment. It is used as the base for dusk calculations that are based on the sun dipping below the horizon after sunset.

Calculated at sea level, without an elevation adjustment.

Non-sunrise and sunset calculations such as dawn and dusk depend on visible light, which is not affected by elevation.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunset

items.title: Sunset
items.text:
The exact sunset time, adjusted for elevation.

Calculated using a geometric zenith of 90° plus an elevation adjustment. The calculation is also adjusted by approximately 50/60 of a degree, accounting for 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total of 90.83333°.

In certain cases, the calculated sunset can occur before sunrise. This typically happens when a time zone other than the local time zone is used; in that case, the sunset date is moved to the following date.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunset

items.title: Sunset Or Westernmost Solar Azimuth
items.text:
Sunset, when sunset occurs. If sunset does not occur that day, this is the time when the sun is at its westernmost position, at azimuth 270°.

If sunset occurs, it uses sunset. If sunset does not occur, it uses the time the sun reaches azimuth 270°. This time is close to six hours after astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

In Polar regions, the Arctic or Antarctic circles, there are days with no sunrise or sunset. There are opinions that during these periods, the day-night boundary is when the sun is at its westernmost position.

In this opinion, sunrise is when the sun is at azimuth 90°, its easternmost position.

This is the opinion of Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳. This is brought down lehalacha by the Ben Ish Chai in רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳.

If there is no sunset that day and azimuth 270° also does not occur, this time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth

items.title: Tzais 120 Minutes
items.text:
Deprecated because it is a very late time that should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar concerns.

Tzais, or nightfall, calculated according to Rav Chaim Naeh’s understanding that a mil according to the Rambam is 24 minutes, with tzais occurring 5 mil after sunset. This is an extremely late time and is intended only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

120 minutes after sunset, or after sea-level sunset depending on the elevation setting.

A similar degree-based calculation uses 26° below the horizon.

This time is long past the 18° point where the darkest point is reached.

In places or dates where the needed sun event cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, this zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais120Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais120Minutes

items.title: Tzais 120 Zmanis
items.text:
Deprecated because it is a very late time that should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar concerns.

Tzais, or dusk, calculated as an extremely late time using 120 zmaniyos minutes after sea-level sunset. It is intended only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

120 zmaniyos minutes after sea-level sunset.

In most places on the globe, this time is when the sun is well below the 18° point, described here as scientifically the darkest point.

In places or dates where the needed sun event cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, this zman may not be available.

items.title: Tzais 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais at the point when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. This corresponds to the sun’s dip below the horizon 72 minutes after sunset according to Rabbeinu Tam’s calculation of tzais around the equinox / equilux in Jerusalem.

Calculated for when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon.

The question of equinox versus equilux is described as complex. Rabbi Meir Posen in the Ohr Meir is cited as favoring the equilux, while Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh and Rabbi Yonah Mertzbuch are cited as favoring the astronomical equinox.

The difference between the equinox and equilux approaches is about 9 seconds, described as too trivial to make much of a difference.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so this zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais16Point1Degrees

Deprecated because it is a very late time that should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar concerns.

Tzais based on when the sun is 26° below the horizon. This is an extremely late time and is intended only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

items.title: Tzais 50 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of Rabbi Moshe Feinstein for the New York area.

50 minutes after sunset, or after sea-level sunset depending on the elevation setting.

This time should not be used for latitudes other than ones similar to the latitude of the New York area.

In places or dates where the needed sun event cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, this zman may not be available.

items.title: Tzais 60 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Chavas Yair and Divrei Malkiel that the time to walk a mil is 15 minutes, for a total of 60 minutes for 4 mil after sunset.

60 minutes after sunset, or after sea-level sunset depending on the elevation setting.

In places or dates where the needed sun event cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, this zman may not be available.

items.title: Tzais 72 Zmanis
items.text:
Tzais, or dusk, according to a calculation described as the Minchas Cohen’s way of calculating Rebbeinu Tam’s time of tzeis.

Calculated as 72 proportional minutes, equal to 1/10 of the day, after sea level sunset.

This calculation makes the time from sunset to tzais shortest at the winter solstice, longest at the summer solstice, and 72 clock minutes at the equinox.

The source notes that this does not match reality, because there is no direct relationship between day length and twilight. It says the shortest twilight is during the equinox, the longest is during the summer solstice, and winter twilight is longer than during the equinoxes despite the shorter daylight.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Zmanis

items.title: Tzais 90 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Magen Avraham that the time to walk a mil according to the Rambam is 18 minutes, with tzais calculated as 5 mil after sunset.

Calculated as 90 minutes after sunset or sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

A similar calculation uses the sun’s position based on this time.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text:

Tzais, or dusk.

Calculated as 96 minutes after sunset or sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

items.text:

items.title: Tzais Ateret Torah
items.text:
Tzais associated with Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah. The source notes that he uses this time only for calculating other daily zmanim such as Sof Zman Krias Shema and Plag Hamincha, and that his calendars do not publish a zman for Tzais.

Usually calculated as 40 minutes after sea level sunset, with the offset configurable to another number of minutes.

Chacham Harari-Raful provided a 25-minute zman for Israel.

The default described here is 40 minutes year-round in any place on the globe.

The time depends on the configured Ateret Torah sunset offset when that setting is changed.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisAteretTorah

items.title: Tzais Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya.

Calculated when the sun is 6° below the western geometric horizon after sunset. This is based on the sun’s position about 24 minutes after sea level sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, rounded up to 6° below geometric zenith. The source also cites About Our Zmanim Calculations @ Chabad.org.

The source describes the calculation as starting from shkiah amitis at 1.583° below the horizon around the equinox / equilux, which computes 3.516 minutes after sunset, then adding 18 minutes for ¾ of a 24-minute mil and two minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

This computes the sun at 5.83° below the horizon, very close to a slightly later 5.95° calculation, and is rounded up to 6°.

The time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisBaalHatanya

items.title: Tzais 72 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on Rabbeinu Tam’s opinion that tzais hakochavim is 72 minutes after sunset, the time it takes to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil.

Calculated as 72 standard clock minutes after sunset or sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

According to the Machtzis Hashekel in Orach Chaim 235:3, the Pri Megadim in Orach Chaim 261:2, and others cited in the source, the 72 minutes are standard clock minutes at any time of year in any location.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Minutes

items.title: Tzais Geonim 3 Point 7 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the point when the sun is 3.7° below the geometric zenith. This corresponds to 13.5 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

It is calculated as ¾ of a mil at 18 minutes per mil, which is 13.5 minutes after sunset, and expressed as the sun being 3.7° below sea level.

This does not include the 26.46 seconds it takes to walk 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of an 18-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh lists 3.65°, which the source says appears to be a drop too early.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees

items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the point when the sun is 3.8° below the geometric zenith. This corresponds to 14 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

It is calculated as ¾ of a mil at 18 minutes per mil, which is 13.5 minutes after sunset, plus 30 seconds for the time to walk 49 amos, for a total of 14 minutes after sunset.

The 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, would take 26.46 seconds at the pace of an 18-minute mil, rounded to 30 seconds.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 4 Point 42 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the sun’s position 4.42° below the western horizon. This is described as a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

It is calculated as ¾ of a mil based on a 22.5-minute mil, which is 16 ⅞ minutes.

This does not include the 33.07 seconds it takes to walk 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 22.5-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh lists 4.37°, which the source says appears to be a drop too early.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon, so this time may not be available or cannot be calculated.

items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the sun’s position 4.66° below the western horizon. This is described as a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

It is calculated as ¾ of a mil based on a 24-minute mil, which is 18 minutes.

This does not include the 35.28 seconds it takes to walk 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 24-minute mil.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 4 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the sun’s position 4.8° below the western horizon. At this point, 3 medium-sized stars are visible to a non-expert with good vision with effort. This is described as an early zman for tzais and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

It is calculated as 18.6 minutes after sunset: ¾ of a 24-minute mil, plus 0.6 minutes for the time to walk 49 amos for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. This corresponds to the sun being 4.8° below the western horizon.

This is based on Rav Yechiel Michel Shlezinger's Aizehu Bain Hashmashos and Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Zmanei Hayom BaHalacha.

At the pace of a 24-minute mil, 49 amos would take 35.28 seconds to walk.

An expert who knows where to look can see the 3 medium stars as early as 15 minutes after sunset.

Hazmanim Bahalacha is cited as explaining that medium-sized stars would be visible as early as 14 minutes after sunset to an expert, based on 13.5 minutes for ¾ of an 18-minute mil plus 0.5 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon, so this time may not be available or cannot be calculated.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 5 Point 95 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, using the point when the sun is 5.95° below the geometric zenith. This is calculated as the sun’s position 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The 24 minutes is based on the Baal Hatanya’s calculation of 18 minutes, which is ¾ of a 24-minute mil, plus 4 minutes for shkiah amitis and 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

Sources cited include Hazmanim Bahalacha and Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol III, ch. 13, no. 53, p. 1026.

Among the sources mentioned for this zman are Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Jewish Chrononomy and other sources.

Calculations show that the time is closer to 5.93° and was seemingly rounded to 5.95°.

Chabad calendars usually use a 6°-based Baal Hatanya time that is built on this same calculation.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh lists 5.88°, which the source says appears to be a drop too early.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon, so this time may not be available or cannot be calculated.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 6 Point 45 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, as calculated by Rabbi Yechiel Michel Tucazinsky. It is described as the commonly used zman in Israel and is also used in the Luach Itim Lebinah.

It is based on the sun’s position no later than 31 minutes after sea-level sunset in Jerusalem. At the height of the summer solstice, 30.75 minutes after shkiah computes to 6.45° below the geometric zenith. The calculation is found in the Birur Halacha Yoreh Deah 262.

The source notes that Birur Halacha shows Rabbi Tucazinsky’s sunset calculations, listed as 28 minutes in this case, were about 3 minutes later than reality.

This differs from the 6.1° or 6.2° calculation for Rabbi Tucazinsky’s time as calculated by Hazmanim Bahalacha.

Calculations show that 6.45° at the equinox is 26.5 minutes after shkiah around the equinox / equilux.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon, so this time may not be available or cannot be calculated.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, when the sun is 7.083°, also written as 7° 5′, below the western horizon.

This calculation is based on the observation of 3 medium-sized stars by Dr. Baruch (Berthold) Cohn in his luach Tabellen enthaltend die Zeitangaben für den Beginn der Nacht und des Tages für die Breitengrade + 66 bis -38, published in Strasbourg, France in 1899.

This calendar was very popular in Europe, and many other calendars based their tzais time on it.

Rav Dovid Tzvi Hoffman, in שו״ת מלמד להועיל, in an exchange of letters with Baruch Cohn in Orach Chaim 30, agreed to this zman, as did the שו״ת בני ציון and the תנובת שדה.

It is very close to the time of the מקור חסד of the Sefer chasidim.

It is close to the sun’s position 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, but not exact. The actual position of the sun 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem is 7.205° at the equilux and 7.199° at the equinox.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon, so this time may not be available or cannot be calculated.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 7 Point 67 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, nightfall, according to the Geonim, based on 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York, when twilight is longest. Sources cited include Igros Moshe Even Haezer 4, Ch. 4 regarding tzais for krias Shema, Rabbi Heber's Shaarei Zmanim, chapter 10 (page 87) and chapter 12 (page 108), Rabbi Simcha Bunim Cohen's The radiance of Shabbos, Divrei Shalom Vol. III, chapter 75, and Bais Av"i Vol. III, chapter 117.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 7.67° below sea level, corresponding to about 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York.

This zman depends on the level of light; Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

In northern and southern locations, even some places south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

items.text:
Tzais, nightfall, based on the calculations used in the Luach Itim Lebinah as the stringent time for tzais.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 9.3° below sea level, below the western horizon.

In northern and southern locations, even some places south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 9 Point 75 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, nightfall, according to the Geonim, based on 60 minutes after sunset around the equinox / equilux, the day that a solar hour is 60 minutes in New York. This is the opinion of Rabbi Eliyahu Henkin and also follows the opinion of Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 9.75° below sea level, corresponding to about 60 minutes after sunset around the equinox or equilux in New York.

Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

In northern and southern locations, even some places south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 8 Point 5 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, nightfall, when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric horizon after sunset. Rabbi Meir Posen in the Ohr Meir calculated this as the time when 3 small stars are visible, which is later than the required 3 medium stars.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric horizon, based on the sun’s position 36 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

In northern and southern locations, even some places south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.
